_meta.keyKnowledge: We are learning that plants and animals have features we can see, and we can sort them into groups.

toLevel2.question: Birds have feathers on their bodies.
toLevel2.correctExplanation: All birds have feathers, which help them fly and stay warm.

distractors.explanation: Some students think only flying birds have feathers, but all birds do.

toLevel2.question: Fish are covered in fur.
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Fish are covered in scales, not fur. Scales help protect them in water.

distractors.explanation: Students may confuse body coverings across animal groups.

toLevel2.question: All mammals have feathers on their bodies.
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Mammals have fur or hair, not feathers. Feathers belong to birds.

distractors.explanation: Students sometimes confuse body coverings between animal groups.

toLevel2.question: Reptiles have wet, slimy skin.
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Reptiles have dry, scaly skin. Frogs and some amphibians have wet skin.

distractors.explanation: Students often confuse reptile skin with amphibian skin.

toLevel2.question: Insects always have six legs.
toLevel2.correctExplanation: All insects have exactly six legs. This is one feature that defines them.

distractors.explanation: Students sometimes confuse insects with spiders, which have eight legs.

toLevel2.question: Spiders are a type of insect.
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Spiders have eight legs and are arachnids. Insects have six legs.

distractors.explanation: Many students think all small crawling creatures are insects.

toLevel2.question: Frogs have smooth, moist skin.
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Frogs are amphibians with smooth, moist skin that helps them breathe.

distractors.explanation: Students may think frogs have scaly skin like reptiles.

toLevel2.question: All animals with wings can fly.
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Some animals have wings but cannot fly, such as emus and penguins.

distractors.explanation: Students often assume wings always mean an animal can fly.

toLevel2.question: Leaves come in different shapes and sizes.
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Plants have leaves of many shapes and sizes, which helps us sort them.

distractors.explanation: Some students think all leaves look the same because they are all green.

toLevel2.question: Snakes have tiny legs hidden under scales.
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Snakes do not have legs. They move by sliding along the ground.

distractors.explanation: Some students believe snakes must have hidden legs to move.

toLevel2.question: A lizard has dry, scaly skin.
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Lizards are reptiles and have dry, scaly skin that protects them.

distractors.explanation: Students may confuse lizard skin with the moist skin of frogs.

toLevel2.question: Flowers on every plant look the same.
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Different plants have flowers of different colours, shapes and sizes.

distractors.explanation: Students may think all flowers are similar because they all grow on plants.

toLevel2.question: Which body covering do fish have?
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Fish have scales that protect their bodies in water.

distractors.explanation: Feathers are found on birds, not fish.
Fur is found on mammals, not fish.

toLevel2.question: How many legs does an insect have?
toLevel2.correctExplanation: All insects have six legs, which is a key feature of the insect group.

distractors.explanation: Four legs belong to many mammals and reptiles, not insects.
Eight legs belong to spiders, which are arachnids, not insects.

toLevel2.question: What type of body covering do birds have?
toLevel2.correctExplanation: All birds have feathers. This is a feature that makes them birds.

distractors.explanation: Scales are found on reptiles and fish, not birds.
Fur is found on mammals, not birds.

toLevel2.question: Which animal group has fur or hair?
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Mammals are the animal group with fur or hair on their bodies.

distractors.explanation: Reptiles have dry scales, not fur or hair.
Birds have feathers, not fur or hair.

toLevel2.question: What kind of skin does a frog have?
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Frogs are amphibians with smooth, moist skin.

distractors.explanation: Dry, scaly skin belongs to reptiles like lizards.
Fur belongs to mammals, not frogs.

toLevel2.question: Which feature helps us sort plants into groups?
toLevel2.correctExplanation: We can sort plants by looking at the shape of their leaves.

distractors.explanation: Soil colour is about where a plant grows, not a plant feature.
Pot size is about how we keep a plant, not a plant feature.

toLevel2.question: Which animal has scales on its body?
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Lizards are reptiles and have dry, scaly skin.

distractors.explanation: Dogs are mammals with fur, not scales.
Parrots are birds with feathers, not scales.

toLevel2.question: What feature do all birds share?
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Feathers are a feature shared by every bird species.

distractors.explanation: Some birds like emus and penguins cannot fly.
Only some birds swim. It is not a shared feature of all birds.

toLevel2.question: Which part of a plant can we use to sort it?
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Flowers come in different shapes and colours and help us sort plants.

distractors.explanation: A shadow is not a feature of the plant itself.
Where a plant grows is not a body feature of the plant.

toLevel2.question: Which animal group has dry, scaly skin?
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Reptiles such as lizards and snakes have dry, scaly skin.

distractors.explanation: Amphibians like frogs have smooth, moist skin.
Mammals have fur or hair, not scales.

toLevel2.question: What do we look at to sort living things?
toLevel2.correctExplanation: We observe features we can see to sort living things into groups.

distractors.explanation: Names are labels, not observable features used for sorting.
Age is hard to observe and is not used to group types of living things.

toLevel2.question: How many legs does a spider have?
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Spiders have eight legs, which makes them different from insects.

distractors.explanation: Six legs belong to insects, not spiders.
No common classroom animal has ten legs.

toLevel2.sentence: Animals with fur are called {{blank:1}}. Animals with feathers are called {{blank:2}}.

blanks.correctExplanation: Mammals are animals that have fur or hair on their bodies.

distractors.explanation: Reptiles have scales, not fur.
Insects have a hard outer shell, not fur.

blanks.correctExplanation: Birds are the only animals that have feathers.

distractors.explanation: Mammals have fur or hair, not feathers.
Fish have scales, not feathers.

toLevel2.sentence: A frog has {{blank:1}} skin. A lizard has {{blank:2}} skin.

blanks.correctExplanation: Frogs are amphibians with smooth, moist skin.

distractors.explanation: Scaly skin belongs to reptiles, not frogs.
Furry skin belongs to mammals, not frogs.

blanks.correctExplanation: Lizards are reptiles with dry, scaly skin.

distractors.explanation: Moist skin belongs to amphibians like frogs, not lizards.
Furry skin belongs to mammals, not lizards.

toLevel3.question: A penguin belongs in the bird group.
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Penguins have feathers and lay eggs, so they are classified as birds.

distractors.explanation: Students think penguins are not birds because they cannot fly.

toLevel3.question: A whale belongs in the fish group.
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Whales are mammals. They breathe air and feed milk to their young.

distractors.explanation: Students often think whales are fish because they live in the ocean.

toLevel3.question: A seahorse belongs in the mammal group.
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Seahorses live in water and have scales, so they are fish.

distractors.explanation: Students may think seahorses are mammals because of the word horse.

toLevel3.question: A bat belongs in the bird group.
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Bats have fur and feed milk to their young, so they are mammals.

distractors.explanation: Students often think bats are birds because they can fly.

toLevel3.question: A dolphin is a type of fish.
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Dolphins are mammals. They breathe air and have smooth skin with no scales.

distractors.explanation: Students often think dolphins are fish because they live in water.

toLevel3.question: We can group plants by their leaf shapes.
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Leaf shape is an observable feature used to sort plants into groups.

distractors.explanation: Students may think plants can only be grouped by flower colour.

toLevel3.question: An emu belongs in the reptile group.
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Emus have feathers and lay eggs, so they are birds.

distractors.explanation: Students may think emus are not birds because they cannot fly.

toLevel3.question: A caterpillar is a young insect.
toLevel3.correctExplanation: A caterpillar is the larval stage of a butterfly or moth, which are insects.

distractors.explanation: Students may think caterpillars are worms rather than young insects.

toLevel3.question: Which is an example of a mammal?
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Dogs have fur and feed milk to their young, which makes them mammals.

distractors.explanation: A goldfish is a fish with scales, not a mammal.
A parrot is a bird with feathers, not a mammal.

toLevel3.question: Why do we sort living things into groups?
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Sorting helps us see patterns and understand the variety of life on Earth.

distractors.explanation: Sorting is about finding patterns, not making things equal.
Sorting is about understanding features, not just counting.

toLevel3.question: Which animal would you put in the reptile group?
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Turtles have dry, scaly skin and lay eggs, so they are reptiles.

distractors.explanation: Frogs have moist skin and are amphibians, not reptiles.
Rabbits have fur and are mammals, not reptiles.

toLevel3.question: Which feature helps you tell a bird from a mammal?
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Birds have feathers while mammals have fur or hair.

distractors.explanation: Both birds and mammals have eyes, so eyes do not help tell them apart.
Both birds and mammals have mouths, so this does not help.

toLevel3.question: How do Aboriginal peoples often group plants?
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples group plants by their uses on Country.

distractors.explanation: Traditional plant knowledge is based on use and place, not price.
Traditional grouping focuses on use and habitat, not age.

toLevel3.question: Which feature would help you sort flowers into groups?
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Counting petals is one way to sort flowers by an observable feature.

distractors.explanation: The garden name is not a feature of the flower itself.
When a flower was picked is not a feature of the flower.

toLevel3.question: Why is a whale classified as a mammal?
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Whales feed milk to their young, which is a key mammal feature.

distractors.explanation: Whales do not have scales. Scales belong to fish and reptiles.
Whales give birth to live young. They do not lay eggs.

toLevel3.question: Which animal is an insect?
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Ants have six legs and three body parts, making them insects.

distractors.explanation: Spiders have eight legs and are arachnids, not insects.
Snails are molluscs with a shell, not insects.

toLevel3.question: How can you tell an amphibian from a reptile?
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Amphibians have moist skin while reptiles have dry, scaly skin.

distractors.explanation: Both groups can be many colours, so colour alone does not help.
Both groups include large and small animals, so size does not help.

toLevel3.question: Which pair of animals belong in the same group?
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Cats and horses are both mammals with fur.

distractors.explanation: A cat is a mammal and an eagle is a bird. They are in different groups.
A cat is a mammal and a salmon is a fish. They are in different groups.

toLevel3.question: What does classifying living things mean?
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Classifying means sorting living things into groups based on shared features.

distractors.explanation: Drawing is recording, not classifying.
Feeding is caring for animals, not classifying them.

toLevel3.question: Which animal would you group with a snake?
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Both snakes and lizards are reptiles with dry, scaly skin.

distractors.explanation: Frogs are amphibians with moist skin, not reptiles.
Worms are invertebrates without scales, not reptiles.

toLevel3.sentence: We can sort animals by looking at their {{blank:1}}. Animals with scales belong to the {{blank:2}} group.

blanks.correctExplanation: We observe features like body covering, legs and shape to sort animals.

distractors.explanation: Names are labels we give animals, not observable features for sorting.
We cannot observe an animal's feelings to sort it into a group.

blanks.correctExplanation: Reptiles are animals with dry, scaly skin.

distractors.explanation: Mammals have fur or hair, not scales.
Birds have feathers, not scales.

toLevel3.sentence: A butterfly is an {{blank:1}} because it has {{blank:2}} legs.

blanks.correctExplanation: Butterflies have six legs and three body parts, so they are insects.

distractors.explanation: Arachnids have eight legs. Butterflies have six.
Amphibians are animals like frogs, not butterflies.

blanks.correctExplanation: All insects have exactly six legs.

distractors.explanation: Eight legs belong to spiders, not insects.
Four legs belong to many mammals and reptiles, not insects.

toLevel3.sentence: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples group plants by how they are {{blank:1}} on {{blank:2}}.

blanks.correctExplanation: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have deep knowledge of how plants are used.

distractors.explanation: Traditional grouping is based on use, not just colour.
Traditional grouping is based on use and habitat, not size alone.

blanks.correctExplanation: Country refers to the land, water and sky that Aboriginal peoples connect to.

distractors.explanation: Traditional knowledge is connected to Country, not written records.
Aboriginal peoples' relationship is with Country, not farms.

toLevel3.sentence: A penguin has {{blank:1}} so it belongs to the {{blank:2}} group.

blanks.correctExplanation: Penguins have waterproof feathers, making them birds.

distractors.explanation: Penguins do not have scales. Scales belong to reptiles and fish.
Penguins do not have fur. Fur belongs to mammals.

blanks.correctExplanation: All animals with feathers are classified as birds.

distractors.explanation: Penguins swim but are birds, not fish.
Penguins are not mammals. They have feathers, not fur.

toLevel3.sentence: Animals that {{blank:1}} in water and have scales are usually {{blank:2}}.

blanks.correctExplanation: Fish are animals that swim in water using fins.

distractors.explanation: Flying is a feature of birds and some insects, not fish.
Hopping is a feature of frogs and kangaroos, not fish.

blanks.correctExplanation: Fish live in water and have scales covering their bodies.

distractors.explanation: Reptiles have scales but most live on land, not in water.
Mammals have fur or hair, not scales.

toLevel3.sentence: We sort living things by their {{blank:1}} to understand the {{blank:2}} of life on Earth.

blanks.correctExplanation: Observable features like body covering and shape help us sort living things.

distractors.explanation: Colour alone is not enough to classify living things into groups.
Sounds are harder to observe and are not the main way we classify.

blanks.correctExplanation: Sorting helps us appreciate the amazing variety of life on Earth.

distractors.explanation: Sorting is about understanding diversity, not how fast things move.
Sorting is about understanding diversity, not how heavy things are.

toLevel4.question: Mia could sort shells by their shape.
toLevel4.correctExplanation: Shape is an observable feature, so Mia can use it to sort shells into groups.

distractors.explanation: Students may think only living things can be sorted by features.

toLevel4.question: Jarrah should group a bat with birds.
toLevel4.correctExplanation: Bats have fur and feed milk to their young, so they belong with mammals.

distractors.explanation: Students think bats are birds because they fly.

toLevel4.question: Anika finds an animal with feathers at the park. What group does it belong to?
toLevel4.correctExplanation: Feathers are the key feature of birds. Any animal with feathers is a bird.

distractors.explanation: Mammals have fur or hair, not feathers.
Reptiles have dry scales, not feathers.

toLevel4.question: Sam sees a small animal with six legs in the garden. What is it?
toLevel4.correctExplanation: Six legs is the key feature of insects.

distractors.explanation: Spiders have eight legs, not six.
Worms have no legs at all.

toLevel4.question: Lila wants to sort leaves she found on a walk. What feature could she use?
toLevel4.correctExplanation: Shape is an observable feature that helps sort leaves into groups.

distractors.explanation: We should never taste unknown plants and taste is not a safe feature.
Leaves are too light to weigh easily in the classroom.

toLevel4.question: Kofi catches a small animal with smooth, wet skin. Which group does it belong to?
toLevel4.correctExplanation: Smooth, moist skin is a key feature of amphibians like frogs.

distractors.explanation: Reptiles have dry, scaly skin, not smooth and wet.
Fish have scales and live in water. They are not found on land.

toLevel4.question: Yen sorts toy animals into groups that fly, swim or walk. What is she doing?
toLevel4.correctExplanation: Sorting things into groups based on features is called classifying.

distractors.explanation: Measuring is finding the size of something, not sorting by features.
Drawing is recording what something looks like, not sorting.

toLevel4.question: Rosa notices some flowers have five petals and others have many. What is she observing?
toLevel4.correctExplanation: The number of petals is an observable feature of plants.

distractors.explanation: Petals belong to flowers, which are parts of plants, not animals.
Petal number is a plant feature, not related to weather.

toLevel4.question: Mia finds a creature with eight legs under a rock. Which group should she choose?
toLevel4.correctExplanation: Animals with eight legs, like spiders, are arachnids.

distractors.explanation: Insects have six legs, not eight.
Reptiles have four legs or none, not eight.

toLevel4.question: Jarrah thinks a dolphin is a fish. Is he correct?
toLevel4.correctExplanation: Dolphins breathe air and feed milk to their young, so they are mammals.

distractors.explanation: Dolphins look like fish but are mammals, not fish.
Dolphins do not have feathers and are not birds.

toLevel4.question: Sam collects leaves of different shapes. How should he sort them into groups?
toLevel4.correctExplanation: Shape is an observable feature that works well for sorting leaves.

distractors.explanation: Leaves do not make distinct sounds for grouping.
Most leaves do not have strong smells for grouping.

toLevel4.question: Anika says an emu is a reptile. Why is she wrong?
toLevel4.correctExplanation: Feathers make emus birds. Reptiles have scales.

distractors.explanation: Emus do not have scales. They have feathers like all birds.
Emus do not have fur. They have feathers.

toLevel4.question: Lila is sorting animals on Country with an Elder. Which feature might she use?
toLevel4.correctExplanation: Aboriginal peoples often group animals by where they live on Country.

distractors.explanation: We cannot always observe what an animal prefers to eat.
Friendliness is not an observable feature for classification.

toLevel4.question: Kofi finds a crab with a hard shell and ten legs. Is it an insect?
toLevel4.correctExplanation: Crabs have ten legs and a hard shell. Insects have six legs.

distractors.explanation: Insects have six legs. A crab has ten legs.
Reptiles have scaly skin. Crabs have a hard shell and are crustaceans.

toLevel4.sentence: Yen finds a creature with {{blank:1}} legs under a log. She decides it is an {{blank:2}}.

blanks.correctExplanation: Six legs is the defining feature of insects.

distractors.explanation: Eight legs would make it an arachnid, not an insect.
Four legs belong to many reptiles and mammals, not insects.

blanks.correctExplanation: Animals with six legs are classified as insects.

distractors.explanation: Arachnids have eight legs, not six.
Amphibians have four legs and moist skin, not six legs.

toLevel4.sentence: Rosa sees a creature with dry, {{blank:1}} skin sunning itself on a rock. She knows it is a {{blank:2}}.

blanks.correctExplanation: Dry, scaly skin is a key feature of reptiles.

distractors.explanation: Furry skin belongs to mammals, not reptiles.
Smooth, moist skin belongs to amphibians, not reptiles.

blanks.correctExplanation: Animals with dry, scaly skin are classified as reptiles.

distractors.explanation: Mammals have fur or hair, not dry scales.
Amphibians have moist skin, not dry scales.

toLevel4.sentence: Mia sorts garden plants by their {{blank:1}} shape. Plants with round leaves go in one group and plants with {{blank:2}} leaves go in another.

blanks.correctExplanation: Leaf shape is an observable feature used to sort plants.

distractors.explanation: Roots are underground and harder to observe for sorting.
Seeds are often inside fruit and not easily observed.

blanks.correctExplanation: Pointy and round are two common leaf shapes used for sorting.

distractors.explanation: Heavy describes weight, not shape.
Loud describes sound, not shape.

toLevel4.sentence: Sam visits the zoo. He sees a {{blank:1}} swimming. It breathes air and feeds {{blank:2}} to its young, so it is a mammal.

blanks.correctExplanation: Whales live in water but are mammals because they breathe air and produce milk.

distractors.explanation: Sharks are fish with gills. They do not breathe air or produce milk.
Turtles are reptiles. They do not feed milk to their young.

blanks.correctExplanation: Feeding milk to young is a defining feature of mammals.

distractors.explanation: Seeds are food for some birds and rodents, not a mammal feature.
Leaves are plant food. Feeding milk is what makes an animal a mammal.

toLevel4.sentence: Jarrah counts the {{blank:1}} on different flowers. Some have five and some have {{blank:2}}. This helps him sort the flowers.

blanks.correctExplanation: Petals are a visible flower feature used for sorting.

distractors.explanation: Roots are underground and not easily counted.
Seeds are inside the flower and not easily counted by looking.

blanks.correctExplanation: Some flowers have many petals, which helps us sort them into a different group.

distractors.explanation: Most common garden flowers have petals.
Most common garden flowers have petals.

toLevel4.sentence: Anika sorts her toy animals. She puts the ones with {{blank:1}} in the bird group and the ones with {{blank:2}} in the mammal group.

blanks.correctExplanation: Feathers are the body covering that defines birds.

distractors.explanation: Scales belong to reptiles and fish, not birds.
Shells belong to turtles and snails, not birds.

blanks.correctExplanation: Fur or hair is the body covering that defines mammals.

distractors.explanation: Feathers belong to birds, not mammals.
Scales belong to reptiles and fish, not mammals.

toLevel4.sentence: Lila notices a frog and a lizard near the pond. The frog has {{blank:1}} skin and the lizard has {{blank:2}} skin.

blanks.correctExplanation: Frogs are amphibians with smooth, moist skin.

distractors.explanation: Scaly skin belongs to reptiles, not frogs.
Furry skin belongs to mammals, not frogs.

blanks.correctExplanation: Lizards are reptiles with dry, scaly skin.

distractors.explanation: Moist skin belongs to amphibians, not lizards.
Feathery covering belongs to birds, not lizards.

toLevel4.sentence: Kofi uses a chart to sort animals at the zoo. Animals that {{blank:1}} go in one column. Animals that {{blank:2}} go in another.

blanks.correctExplanation: Flying is one way to sort animals by how they move.

distractors.explanation: Animals do not talk. This is not a useful sorting feature.
Animals do not read. This is not an observable animal feature.

blanks.correctExplanation: Swimming is another way animals move, useful for sorting.

distractors.explanation: Singing is not a standard way to sort animals into groups.
Animals do not cook. This is not an observable feature.

toLevel4.sentence: Rosa learns that Aboriginal peoples know many {{blank:1}} on Country. They group animals by where they {{blank:2}}.

blanks.correctExplanation: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have detailed knowledge of local animals.

distractors.explanation: Machines are not living things found on Country.
Buildings are not living things found on Country.

blanks.correctExplanation: Aboriginal peoples often classify animals by their habitat on Country.

distractors.explanation: Where animals sleep is only part of habitat, not the main grouping.
Play is a human concept and not used for classifying animals.

toLevel4.sentence: Yen draws a picture of a {{blank:1}} with wings. Even though it does not {{blank:2}}, it is still a bird.

blanks.correctExplanation: Penguins are birds that have wings but cannot fly.

distractors.explanation: Goldfish are fish without wings.
Rabbits are mammals without wings.

blanks.correctExplanation: Penguins cannot fly, but they are still birds because they have feathers.

distractors.explanation: Penguins can swim very well. It is flying they cannot do.
Penguins can walk. It is flying they cannot do.

toLevel4.sentence: Mia compares two insects. One has {{blank:1}} and one does not. Both have six {{blank:2}}.

blanks.correctExplanation: Some insects have wings and some do not, but all have six legs.

distractors.explanation: Scales belong to fish and reptiles, not insects.
Fur belongs to mammals, not insects.

blanks.correctExplanation: All insects have six legs, whether or not they have wings.

distractors.explanation: Insects do not have tails. Six legs is their key feature.
Insects have legs, not arms.

toLevel4.sentence: Sam sorts {{blank:1}} by counting their petals. Daisies have {{blank:2}} petals than tulips.

blanks.correctExplanation: Flowers can be sorted by counting their petals.

distractors.explanation: Rocks do not have petals and are not living things.
Animals do not have petals. Petals belong to flowers.

blanks.correctExplanation: Daisies have many thin petals while tulips have fewer large petals.

distractors.explanation: Daisies actually have many more petals than tulips.
Thicker describes petal width, not petal number.

toLevel5.question: Mia calls a whale a fish. Jarrah calls it a mammal. Who is correct?
toLevel5.correctExplanation: Whales breathe air and feed milk to their young, making them mammals.

distractors.explanation: Swimming alone does not make an animal a fish. Body features matter more.
A whale can only be in one group. It is a mammal, not a fish.

toLevel5.question: Anika sorts animals by colour. Sam sorts by body covering. Which works better?
toLevel5.correctExplanation: Body covering tells us about the animal group. Colour varies widely within groups.

distractors.explanation: Colour varies too much within a group to be useful for classifying.
Body covering is a reliable feature for grouping animals.

toLevel5.question: Lila says a bat is a bird because it flies. Why is she wrong?
toLevel5.correctExplanation: Bats have fur and feed milk, so they are mammals. Flying does not make an animal a bird.

distractors.explanation: Bats do not have scales. They have fur, which makes them mammals.
Bats do not lay eggs. They give birth to live young.

toLevel5.question: Kofi says all water animals are fish. What is wrong with his idea?
toLevel5.correctExplanation: Dolphins and whales live in water but are mammals, not fish.

distractors.explanation: Fish do live in water, but so do some mammals.
Many animals live in water, including fish and mammals.

toLevel5.question: One animal has feathers and another has fur. Can they be in the same group?
toLevel5.correctExplanation: Feathers mean bird and fur means mammal. They belong in different groups.

distractors.explanation: Animals with different body coverings belong in different groups.
Size does not decide an animal's group. Body covering does.

toLevel5.question: Yen says a penguin is a mammal because it walks. What proves her wrong?
toLevel5.correctExplanation: Penguins have feathers, which means they are birds, even though they walk on land.

distractors.explanation: Penguins do not have fur. They have feathers.
Penguins do not have scales. They have feathers.

toLevel5.question: Sam sorts leaves by shape instead of where he found them. Why is shape better?
toLevel5.correctExplanation: Shape is a feature of the leaf itself. Where it was found is not a plant feature.

distractors.explanation: Location is about the environment, not the leaf itself.
A feature of the plant is more useful than where it happened to be found.

toLevel5.question: Aboriginal peoples group plants by use. Scientists group them by features. Why are both valuable?
toLevel5.correctExplanation: Both systems organise plants in useful ways using different perspectives.

distractors.explanation: Both grouping systems are valid and useful for different purposes.
Both systems are effective ways to organise plant knowledge.

toLevel5.question: Mia says a spider is an insect. What observation would correct her?
toLevel5.correctExplanation: Spiders have eight legs and insects have six. Counting legs shows the difference.

distractors.explanation: Colour does not tell us if something is an insect or an arachnid.
Size does not tell us if something is an insect or an arachnid.

toLevel5.question: Jarrah says body covering is better than size for grouping animals. Do you agree?
toLevel5.correctExplanation: Body covering reliably tells us which group an animal belongs to. Size varies too much.

distractors.explanation: Size varies within every animal group, so it is not reliable for grouping.
Body covering is a reliable feature for classifying animals.

toLevel5.question: Anika calls a snake an amphibian because of its smooth skin. Why is she wrong?
toLevel5.correctExplanation: Snakes have dry, scaly skin. Amphibians have moist skin. Snakes are reptiles.

distractors.explanation: Snakes do not have fur. They have dry scales.
Snakes do not have feathers. They have dry scales.

toLevel5.question: A frog and lizard are both small and green. Why are they in different groups?
toLevel5.correctExplanation: Frogs have moist skin (amphibian) and lizards have dry scales (reptile).

distractors.explanation: Both can be green. Colour is not the feature that separates their groups.
Both can be similar sizes. Skin type is what matters for grouping.

toLevel5.question: Kofi notices that classifying animals uses the same thinking as sorting shapes in maths. Why?
toLevel5.correctExplanation: Classifying in science and sorting in maths both involve grouping by shared features.

distractors.explanation: Classifying animals does not always involve numbers.
Both science and maths use the skill of sorting by features.

toLevel5.question: Rosa wants to prove a platypus is a mammal. Which evidence is strongest?
toLevel5.correctExplanation: Feeding milk to young is the defining feature of mammals.

distractors.explanation: Having a beak might suggest it is a bird, but feeding milk makes it a mammal.
Swimming does not tell us if an animal is a mammal. Many groups swim.

toLevel5.sentence: A whale lives in the ocean but it is a {{blank:1}} because it breathes {{blank:2}} and feeds milk to its young.

blanks.correctExplanation: Whales are mammals even though they live in water.

distractors.explanation: Fish breathe through gills, not lungs. Whales breathe air.
Reptiles have scaly skin and do not feed milk to young.

blanks.correctExplanation: Whales come to the surface to breathe air, unlike fish.

distractors.explanation: Fish absorb oxygen from water, but whales breathe air.
No animal breathes sand. Whales breathe air.

toLevel5.sentence: Sorting by {{blank:1}} covering is more reliable than sorting by {{blank:2}} because body covering defines each group.

blanks.correctExplanation: Body covering such as fur, feathers or scales defines animal groups.

distractors.explanation: Cloud covering is about weather, not animal classification.
Ground covering is about the environment, not animal features.

blanks.correctExplanation: Colour varies within every group, so it is not reliable for classifying.

distractors.explanation: Feathers are a type of body covering, which is actually reliable for sorting.
Scales are a type of body covering, which is actually reliable for sorting.

toLevel5.sentence: A penguin cannot {{blank:1}} but it has feathers, so scientists classify it as a {{blank:2}}.

blanks.correctExplanation: Penguins have wings but cannot fly. They are still birds.

distractors.explanation: Penguins are excellent swimmers.
Penguins can walk. It is flying they cannot do.

blanks.correctExplanation: Having feathers is what makes an animal a bird, not whether it can fly.

distractors.explanation: Penguins swim well but have feathers, so they are birds, not fish.
Penguins have feathers, not fur, so they are birds, not mammals.

toLevel5.sentence: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have {{blank:1}} knowledge of local plants and animals, grouping them by how they are {{blank:2}} and where they live.

blanks.correctExplanation: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples hold deep, detailed ecological knowledge.

distractors.explanation: Aboriginal knowledge is rich and detailed, not limited.
Aboriginal ecological knowledge is complex and detailed.

blanks.correctExplanation: Aboriginal peoples group plants and animals by their practical uses on Country.

distractors.explanation: Drawing is one way to record knowledge, but grouping is by use.
Naming is part of knowledge, but the grouping is based on use.

toLevel5.sentence: A bat has {{blank:1}} and feeds milk, so it is a mammal. Flying does not make it a {{blank:2}}.

blanks.correctExplanation: Bats have fur, which is a key mammal feature.

distractors.explanation: Bats do not have feathers. Feathers belong to birds.
Bats do not have scales. Scales belong to reptiles and fish.

blanks.correctExplanation: Birds are defined by feathers, not by flying. Bats fly but are mammals.

distractors.explanation: A bat is already a mammal. Flying does not change its group.
Flying does not make a bat a reptile. Bats are mammals.

toLevel5.sentence: Classifying is a thinking {{blank:1}} we use in science, maths and {{blank:2}} life.

blanks.correctExplanation: Classifying is a transferable thinking skill used across many areas.

distractors.explanation: Classifying is a skill, not a game.
Classifying is a genuine skill, not a trick.

blanks.correctExplanation: We classify things in everyday life, like sorting laundry or groceries.

distractors.explanation: We use classifying all the time, not only on holidays.
We use classifying every day, not only on weekends.

toLevel5.sentence: A frog and a lizard both live near a pond. The frog is an {{blank:1}} and the lizard is a {{blank:2}} because their skin is different.

blanks.correctExplanation: Frogs have smooth, moist skin, which makes them amphibians.

distractors.explanation: Reptiles have dry, scaly skin. Frogs have moist skin.
Insects have six legs and a hard outer shell. Frogs are amphibians.

blanks.correctExplanation: Lizards have dry, scaly skin, which makes them reptiles.

distractors.explanation: Amphibians have moist skin. Lizards have dry, scaly skin.
Mammals have fur. Lizards have scales.

toLevel5.sentence: When we compare a spider and an ant, we see the spider has {{blank:1}} legs and the ant has {{blank:2}} legs. They belong to different groups.

blanks.correctExplanation: Spiders are arachnids with eight legs.

distractors.explanation: Six legs belong to insects like ants, not spiders.
Spiders have eight legs, not ten.

blanks.correctExplanation: Ants are insects with six legs.

distractors.explanation: Eight legs belong to arachnids like spiders, not insects.
Ants have six legs, not four.

toLevel5.sentence: Scientists and Aboriginal peoples both {{blank:1}} living things into groups. Each system helps us {{blank:2}} the natural world.

blanks.correctExplanation: Both systems organise living things into groups based on different criteria.

distractors.explanation: Classifying is about organising, not hiding things.
Classifying is about organising, not removing things.

blanks.correctExplanation: Sorting living things helps us understand the variety and patterns in nature.

distractors.explanation: Classification helps us learn about the natural world, not ignore it.
Classification helps us remember and understand, not forget.

toLevel5.sentence: A platypus has a {{blank:1}} like a bird but it feeds {{blank:2}} to its young, so it is a mammal.

blanks.correctExplanation: The platypus has a beak-like bill, which can be confusing for classification.

distractors.explanation: Platypuses do not have wings. They have a beak-like bill.
Many animals have tails. The beak is the unusual bird-like feature.

blanks.correctExplanation: Feeding milk is the key feature that makes the platypus a mammal.

distractors.explanation: Seeds are food for birds, not a mammal feature.
Feeding milk, not water, defines a mammal.

toLevel5.sentence: Sorting by {{blank:1}} is useful in science and in everyday life. At home we sort {{blank:2}} into groups too.

blanks.correctExplanation: Sorting by features is a skill we use in science and daily life.

distractors.explanation: We cannot sort objects by feelings. Features are observable.
Wishes are not observable and cannot be used for sorting.

blanks.correctExplanation: Sorting clothes by colour or type is an everyday classifying activity.

distractors.explanation: Dreams are not physical things we can sort into groups.
Thoughts are not physical things we can sort into groups.

toLevel5.sentence: Two animals look similar but one has {{blank:1}} skin and the other has {{blank:2}} skin. They are in different groups.

blanks.correctExplanation: Moist skin is a feature of amphibians like frogs.

distractors.explanation: Animals do not have metal skin. Moist skin belongs to amphibians.
Animals do not have wooden skin. Moist skin belongs to amphibians.

blanks.correctExplanation: Scaly skin is a feature of reptiles like lizards.

distractors.explanation: Animals do not have plastic skin. Scaly skin belongs to reptiles.
Animals do not have paper skin. Scaly skin belongs to reptiles.
